Your Rocket Mortgage payment is $3,850 per month — the single largest expense in your budget, representing about 42% of total spending in May. That's a meaningful amount to examine, and the good news is there are several low-risk angles worth exploring before considering anything drastic.
Start with what you can verify today
The first step is understanding what's inside that $3,850 payment. According to your transaction notes, it covers principal, interest, taxes, and insurance (PITI) for your Denver home. Each component has different levers:
Principal + interest: This is the loan portion — the only part directly affected by refinancing or term changes.
Property taxes: Set by Denver/Colorado county assessors. You can appeal if you believe your home is over-assessed, which happens more often than people realize after market corrections.
Homeowners insurance: Entirely shoppable. Rates vary significantly across carriers for the same coverage. Given you're already with State Farm (paying $154/month separately), it's worth confirming whether your homeowners insurance is bundled into the Rocket Mortgage escrow or billed separately — if it's in both places, that's worth clarifying.
The refinancing question
Since this involves tax considerations, I'll explain educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Current 30-year fixed rates are around 6.48% as of early June 2026, down from 6.85% a year ago. Whether refinancing makes sense depends on three things:
What rate you currently have. If you're already at or below 6%, refinancing likely doesn't help. If you're above 7%, it may be worth a closer look.
Break-even period. Closing costs typically run 2–5% of the loan amount. On a large Denver mortgage, that could be $15,000–$25,000 or more upfront — meaning you'd need to stay in the home long enough for monthly savings to recover that cost (often 24–36 months at minimum).
Remaining term. Resetting to a new 30-year loan lowers the monthly payment but extends the total interest you pay over time — so it's a cash-flow improvement that may cost more long-term.
A rate reduction of at least 0.75% with a break-even under 36 months is a reasonable threshold for refinancing to make sense.
Lower-risk steps to explore first
PMI removal: If your home has appreciated and your loan-to-value ratio has dropped below 80%, you may be able to request PMI removal if it applies — that can reduce the monthly obligation without any refinancing.
Insurance shopping: Your State Farm auto and home insurance together run $372/month. Bundling or re-quoting with multiple carriers could surface meaningful savings.
Escrow review: Lenders sometimes over-collect for taxes and insurance in escrow. Request an escrow analysis — if there's a surplus, you may be owed a refund or a payment reduction.
Property tax appeal: Denver home values have softened slightly in 2026. If your assessed value hasn't adjusted, an appeal could reduce the tax portion of your PITI.
The most important first step is pulling your current mortgage statement to confirm your exact interest rate and remaining balance — that single number determines whether refinancing is even worth modeling. Do you have that detail handy?
